From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Elements of Chinese culture purged during Mao's Cultural Revolution

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Wanlitod.jpg/250px-Wanlitod.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wanlitod.jpg)

The remains of the [Wanli Emperor](/wiki/Wanli_Emperor "Wanli Emperor") (r. 1572–1620) at the [Ming dynasty tombs](/wiki/Ming_dynasty_tombs "Ming dynasty tombs"). Red Guards dragged the remains of the emperor and of his empresses to the front of the tomb, where they denounced and burned them.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The **Four Olds** ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 四旧; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 四舊; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *sì jiù*) refer to categories used by the [Red Guards](/wiki/Red_Guards "Red Guards") during the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution") to characterize elements of [Chinese culture](/wiki/Chinese_culture "Chinese culture") prior to the [Chinese Communist Revolution](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution") that they were attempting to destroy. The Four Olds were 'old ideas', 'old culture', 'old customs', and 'old habits'.[[a]](#cite_note-2)[[2]](#cite_note-Spence-3) During the [Red August](/wiki/Red_August "Red August") of 1966, shortly after the onset of the Cultural Revolution, the Red Guards' campaign to destroy the Four Olds began amid the massacres being carried out in Beijing.[[3]](#cite_note-Wang-2001-4)[[4]](#cite_note-Law-5)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Four_Olds&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

The term "Four Olds" first appeared on 1 June 1966, in [Chen Boda](/wiki/Chen_Boda "Chen Boda")'s *[People's Daily](/wiki/People%27s_Daily "People's Daily")* editorial, "[Sweep Away All Cow Demons and Snake Spirits](/wiki/Sweep_Away_All_Cow_Demons_and_Snake_Spirits "Sweep Away All Cow Demons and Snake Spirits")", where the Old Things were described as anti-[proletarian](/wiki/Proletarian "Proletarian"), "fostered by the exploiting classes, [and to] have poisoned the minds of the people for thousands of years".[[5]](#cite_note-Li-6) However, which customs, cultures, habits, and ideas specifically constituted the "Four Olds" were never clearly defined.[[6]](#cite_note-Lu-7)

On 8 August, the [Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") used the term at its [8th National Congress](/wiki/8th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "8th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party"). The term was endorsed on 18 August by [Lin Biao](/wiki/Lin_Biao "Lin Biao") at a mass rally, and from there it spread to *[Red Flag](/wiki/Red_Flag_(magazine) "Red Flag (magazine)")* magazine, as well as to Red Guard publications.[[6]](#cite_note-Lu-7) During this time, changes associated with the destruction of the Four Olds included: renaming places and people with "revolutionary names", destruction of historical and religious sites and texts, and the arrest of clergy.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10)

Calls to destroy the "Four Olds" usually did not appear in isolation, but were contrasted with the hope of building the "Four News" (new customs, new culture, new habits, new ideas).[[6]](#cite_note-Lu-7) [Newborn socialist things](/wiki/Newborn_socialist_things "Newborn socialist things") were said to struggle against the Four Olds.[[10]](#cite_note-11) The idea that [Chinese culture](/wiki/Chinese_culture "Chinese culture") was responsible for China's economic backwardness and needed to be reformed had some precedent in the [May Fourth Movement](/wiki/May_Fourth_Movement "May Fourth Movement") (1919), and was also encouraged by colonial authorities during the [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War").[[11]](#cite_note-Gao-12)

## Campaign to destroy the Four Olds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Four_Olds&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Campaign to destroy the Four Olds")]

See also: [Red August](/wiki/Red_August "Red August") and [Five Black Categories](/wiki/Five_Black_Categories "Five Black Categories")

The campaign to Destroy the Four Olds and Cultivate the Four News (Chinese: 破四旧立四新; pinyin: *Pò Sìjiù Lì Sìxīn*) began in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") on 19 August during the "[Red August](/wiki/Red_August "Red August")".[[5]](#cite_note-Li-6)[[12]](#cite_note-13) Academic Alessandro Russo writes that the destruction of the Four Olds was an ambiguous campaign from the perspective of the [Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party "Chinese Communist Party").[[13]](#cite_note-Russo-2020-14) He argues that in a time of increasing political pluralization, the Party sought to channel student activism towards obvious class enemies and less relevant objectives to make it easier for the Party to contain the situation.[[13]](#cite_note-Russo-2020-14)

### The "re-naming" campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Four_Olds&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The \"re-naming\" campaign")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Beijing_1968_I.jpg/500px-Beijing_1968_I.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beijing_1968_I.jpg)

A 1968 map of Beijing showing streets and landmarks renamed during the Cultural Revolution. "Āndìngménnèidàjiē" (Stability Gate Inner Street) became "Dàyuèjìnlù" (Great Leap Forward Road), "Táijīchǎngdàjiē" (Taiji Factory Street) became "Yǒnggélù" (Perpetually Ousting Road), "Dōngjiāomínxiàng" (East Cross People's Lane) was renamed "Fǎndìlù" (Anti-Imperialist Road), "Běihǎigōngyuán" (North Sea Park) was renamed "Gōngnóngbīnggōngyuán" (Worker-Peasant-Soldier Park) and "Jǐngshāngōngyuán" (Mountain View Park) became "Hóngwèibīnggōngyuán" (Red Guards Park). Most of the Cultural Revolution-era name changes were later reversed.

Across China, signs bearing old road names were vandalized and renamed.[[14]](#cite_note-Howlett-2022-15)[[15]](#cite_note-Ou-2016-16) The first things to change were the names of streets and stores: "[Blue Sky](/wiki/Blue_Sky_with_a_White_Sun "Blue Sky with a White Sun") Clothes Store" to "Defending [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") Clothes Store", "[Cai E](/wiki/Cai_E "Cai E") Road" to "Red Guards Road", and so forth.

In Beijing, the name of the road where the embassy of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") was stationed was changed to "[Anti-revisionism](/wiki/Anti-revisionism "Anti-revisionism") Road".[[13]](#cite_note-Russo-2020-14) The [Peking Union Medical College Hospital](/wiki/Peking_Union_Medical_College_Hospital "Peking Union Medical College Hospital"), founded in 1921 by the [Rockefeller Foundation](/wiki/Rockefeller_Foundation "Rockefeller Foundation"), was renamed "Anti-Imperialist Hospital".[[16]](#cite_note-17)

In [Huangpu district](/wiki/Huangpu_District,_Shanghai "Huangpu District, Shanghai") of [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), the city's commercial center, Red Guards tore down 93 percent of shop signboards (2,166 of 2,328), and renamed restaurants, schools and hospitals.[[14]](#cite_note-Howlett-2022-15) Red Guards also took [Nanjing Road](/wiki/Nanjing_Road "Nanjing Road") as their revolutionary headquarters in Shanghai, renaming it the "Anti-Imperialism Street".[[14]](#cite_note-Howlett-2022-15)

Many people across China also changed their [given names](/wiki/Given_name "Given name") to revolutionary slogans, such as Zhihong (志红, "Determined Red"), Jige (继革, "Following the Revolution") and Weidong (卫东, "Safeguard [the Orient](/wiki/The_Orient "The Orient") or Protect Mao").[[6]](#cite_note-Lu-7)[[15]](#cite_note-Ou-2016-16)[[17]](#cite_note-18)

### Public sites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Four_Olds&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Public sites")]

See also: [Antireligious campaigns of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Antireligious_campaigns_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Antireligious campaigns of the Chinese Communist Party")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a1/Gate_of_Divine_Might_in_the_Cultural_Revolution.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gate_of_Divine_Might_in_the_Cultural_Revolution.jpg)

The [Forbidden City](/wiki/Forbidden_City "Forbidden City") in Beijing was renamed the "Palace of Blood and Tears".[[18]](#cite_note-19)

The [Cemetery of Confucius](/wiki/Cemetery_of_Confucius "Cemetery of Confucius") was attacked in November 1966, during the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"), when it was visited and vandalized by a team of Red Guards from [Beijing Normal University](/wiki/Beijing_Normal_University "Beijing Normal University"), led by Tan Houlan.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) The corpse of the 76th-generation [Duke Yansheng](/wiki/Duke_Yansheng "Duke Yansheng") (a descendant of Confucius) was removed from its grave and hung naked from a tree in front of the palace during the desecration of the cemetery in the Cultural Revolution.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

The Chinese government stopped short of endorsing the physical destruction of products. In fact, the government protected significant archaeological discoveries made during the Cultural Revolution, such as the [Mawangdui](/wiki/Mawangdui "Mawangdui"), the [Leshan Giant Buddha](/wiki/Leshan_Giant_Buddha "Leshan Giant Buddha") and the [Terracotta Army](/wiki/Terracotta_Army "Terracotta Army").[[11]](#cite_note-Gao-12) Upon learning that Red Guards were approaching the [Forbidden City](/wiki/Forbidden_City "Forbidden City"), Premier [Zhou Enlai](/wiki/Zhou_Enlai "Zhou Enlai") ordered the gates shut immediately and deployed the [People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") against the Red Guards. After this incident, Zhou attempted to create a more peaceful [code of conduct](/wiki/Code_of_conduct "Code of conduct") for the Red Guards, with the support of cadres [Tao Zhu](/wiki/Tao_Zhu "Tao Zhu"), Li Fuchuan, and [Chen Yi](/wiki/Chen_Yi_(communist) "Chen Yi (communist)"). This plan was foiled by the ultra-leftists [Kang Sheng](/wiki/Kang_Sheng "Kang Sheng"), [Jiang Qing](/wiki/Jiang_Qing "Jiang Qing"), and [Zhang Chunqiao](/wiki/Zhang_Chunqiao "Zhang Chunqiao"). Although many of Zhou's other initiatives to stem the destruction failed because of their or Mao's own opposition, he did succeed in preventing Beijing from being renamed "[East Is Red](/wiki/The_East_Is_Red_(song) "The East Is Red (song)") City" and the [Chinese guardian lions](/wiki/Chinese_guardian_lions "Chinese guardian lions") in front of [Tian'anmen Square](/wiki/Tian%27anmen_Square "Tian'anmen Square") from being replaced with statues of Mao.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

In later stages of the campaign, examples of [Chinese architecture](/wiki/Chinese_architecture "Chinese architecture") were destroyed, classical [literature](/wiki/Chinese_literature "Chinese literature") and [Chinese paintings](/wiki/Chinese_painting "Chinese painting") were torn apart, and [Chinese temples](/wiki/Chinese_temple "Chinese temple") were desecrated.[[6]](#cite_note-Lu-7)

### Personal harassment and private properties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Four_Olds&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Personal harassment and private properties")]

Other manifestations of the Red Guard campaign included giving speeches, posting [big-character posters](/wiki/Big-character_poster "Big-character poster"), and harassment of people, such as [intellectuals](/wiki/Intellectual "Intellectual"),[[23]](#cite_note-Wen-24) who defiantly demonstrated the Four Olds.[[5]](#cite_note-Li-6) This escalated from accosting people in the streets due to their dress or hairstyle, to widespread murder, assault, arbitrary detention and the ransacking of private homes.[[14]](#cite_note-Howlett-2022-15) Red Guards broke into the homes of the wealthy and destroyed paintings, books, and furniture; all were items that they viewed as part of the Four Olds.[[24]](#cite_note-Kort-25)

Many artists and other cultural professionals were persecuted by vigilantes, although some cultural advances came about because of the period, including the integration of "new" western instruments and [ballet](/wiki/Ballet "Ballet") into [Peking opera](/wiki/Peking_opera "Peking opera").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Attacks on ethnic minorities and book burnings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Four_Olds&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Attacks on ethnic minorities and book burnings")]

Languages and customs of [ethnic minorities in China](/wiki/Ethnic_minorities_in_China "Ethnic minorities in China") were labeled as part of the Four Olds and texts in ethnic languages were burned.[[25]](#cite_note-Qingxia-2001-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27)[[27]](#cite_note-28) Bilingual education was suppressed.[[25]](#cite_note-Qingxia-2001-26)

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Four_Olds&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

### Appraisal of damage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Four_Olds&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Appraisal of damage")]

See also: [Boluan Fanzheng](/wiki/Boluan_Fanzheng "Boluan Fanzheng")

No official statistics have ever been produced by the Communist party in terms of reporting the actual cost of damage. By 1978, many stories of death and destruction caused by the Cultural Revolution had leaked out of China and became known worldwide.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

### Preservation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Four_Olds&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Preservation")]

During and after the Cultural Revolution, efforts were made to protect Chinese cultural artifacts. Shanghai officials intervened in Red Guard house searches, relocating items to safety and documenting those that couldn't be moved for future restoration.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Post-Cultural Revolution, there was a renewed effort to preserve cultural heritage, with initiatives like the Lost Cultural Relics Recovery Program and the establishment of the State Administration of Cultural Heritage to protect and manage historical sites and artifacts.[[32]](#cite_note-33)
